planting.note: Nick seed coat with sandpaper or soak overnight before sowing directly in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil after last frost; do not start indoors as tap root is damaged by transplanting.

directions: Nick seed coat with sandpaper or soak overnight before sowing directly in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil after last frost; do not start indoors as tap root is damaged by transplanting. Ipomoea tricolor is the parent of the famous 'Heavenly Blue' morning glory, a fast-growing annual twining vine with heart-shaped leaves and large (3–4 inch), funnel-shaped flowers that open a vivid sky-blue or blue-violet each morning and fade to pink-purple by afternoon — one of the most startling and beautiful morning colors in the annual garden. Provide a trellis, fence, or other support to 8–12 feet; it will naturally scramble up any available structure. The seeds contain lysergic acid amides and are mildly toxic if eaten in quantity. Morning glories bloom most prolifically in lean, dry soil with minimal fertilization; rich soil produces excessive foliage.

planting.note: Nick or soak seeds overnight in warm water before direct sowing after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun; cypress vine self-seeds prolifically in warm climates and can become invasive — deadhead if naturalization is not desired.

directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight in warm water before direct sowing after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun; cypress vine self-seeds prolifically in warm climates and can become invasive — deadhead if naturalization is not desired. Ipomoea quamoclit is a delicate, fast-growing annual vine from tropical America with extraordinarily fine, feathery, deeply pinnate leaves resembling cypress foliage and a long succession of small (1-inch), brilliant scarlet, star-shaped flowers with a white eye from summer through frost that are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds. In zone 9–10 it self-seeds vigorously and can naturalize along fences and into disturbed areas; it has been listed as invasive in some southeastern states, so monitor seedling spread and remove volunteers if they escape the garden area. An outstanding container or trellis plant for attracting hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 3–4 inches deep in spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; the rhizomes prefer cool soil — keep them shaded even when the top growth reaches into the sun; in hot summers the plant may go dormant.

directions: Plant rhizomes 3–4 inches deep in spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; the rhizomes prefer cool soil — keep them shaded even when the top growth reaches into the sun; in hot summers the plant may go dormant. Tropaeolum speciosum is the spectacular perennial climbing nasturtium from the cool, moist forests of Chile, producing long, twining stems with small, deeply lobed, bright-green leaves and a seemingly endless succession of vivid scarlet-red, spurred nasturtium flowers followed by striking, blue-black berries surrounded by bright red sepals from summer through early fall. It is famously beautiful scrambling through dark evergreen shrubs like yew or rhododendron in cool, moist British gardens, but is challenging in hot climates — it requires cool soil, consistent moisture, and partial shade and goes dormant in heat. A collector's plant for the cool, moist garden.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society Award of Garden Merit

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 4 weeks before) in well-drained, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; canary creeper prefers cooler conditions than garden nasturtium (T. majus) and blooms most freely in partial shade and cool summers.

directions: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 4 weeks before) in well-drained, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; canary creeper prefers cooler conditions than garden nasturtium (T. majus) and blooms most freely in partial shade and cool summers. Tropaeolum peregrinum is a vigorous annual climbing vine from the Andean cloud forests of Peru with deeply five-lobed, gray-green leaves and profuse, small (3/4 inch), bright-golden-yellow flowers with two large, fringed upper petals that create an extraordinary resemblance to small yellow canaries or birds-in-flight, giving the plant its charming common name. It is an outstanding annual climber for a partially shaded trellis, fence, or pergola and is useful where full-sun annuals fail; it provides nectar for hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees. Less commonly grown than garden nasturtium but more refined and tolerant of shade and cooler conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or under glass 4 weeks before) in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, poor to average, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; dwarf morning glory does not like to be transplanted — direct sow where it is to grow and thin to 9–12 inches.

directions: Direct sow after last frost (or under glass 4 weeks before) in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, poor to average, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; dwarf morning glory does not like to be transplanted — direct sow where it is to grow and thin to 9–12 inches. Convolvulus tricolor is a bushy, non-climbing annual with satiny, funnel-shaped flowers uniquely marked with three concentric bands of color — vivid blue on the outside, white in the middle, and golden-yellow at the center with a white eye — that remain open all day (unlike true morning glories, which close by afternoon). This unusual all-day flowering habit makes it more practical for busy gardeners and for areas where morning shade would close a true morning glory. It is compact and bushy rather than vining and makes an outstanding front-of-border or container plant for hot, dry positions; flowers attract small native bees and hoverflies.
